champion.narrative: Melissa Geddie (Sr. Manager, Procurement, Evaluator) is the only contact and was developing as a potential champion through early March 2026, but she has now been unresponsive for 100 days (last contact 2026-03-23). Her role is operational (Sr. Manager), not executive, so she does not control the $90k budget and cannot sign off on the deal without CFO or CPO approval. In the 2026-03-23 task she committed to forming an evaluation committee and scheduling discovery and demo, signaling intent and some internal advocacy, but neither action materialized. The 2026-07-01 Next Step shows the rep sent a probing email to gauge project priority, but there has been no reply. Melissa has not introduced us to the Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or any other stakeholder. She has not shared internal docs (RFP, BRD, evaluation scorecard), pushed back on competitors on our behalf, or driven the agenda forward. Her engagement has gone from active (March) to silent (100 days), signaling either deprioritization of the project, political roadblocks internally (budget pulled, competing priorities), or selection of another vendor without informing us. Without a response within 7 days of the 2026-07-01 outreach, Melissa is no longer a viable champion. If she does re-engage, the first move is to assess her current influence and access: 'Where does this project sit on your priority list today? Who else internally is driving the evaluation, and can you introduce me to the budget owner?'

competition.narrative: Four competitors are confirmed in a multi-vendor evaluation: Coupa, GEP Worldwide, iValua, and JAGGAER (source: Salesforce Competitors__c field). All four are credible enterprise procurement platforms with overlapping CLM, sourcing, P2P, and spend analytics capabilities. With zero discovery, zero demo, and 100 days dark, we have no competitive intelligence: no idea which vendor is ahead, what differentiation the buyer values, or where Zycus stands in the shortlist. The buyer may have already selected a vendor and simply not informed us. Coupa is the likely front-runner (market leader in procurement, strong P2P and spend management, law-firm customer base). GEP Worldwide and JAGGAER are strong in sourcing and supplier collaboration. iValua has a solid CLM offering. Zycus's wedge is Merlin AI (agentic negotiation intelligence, supplier price history analytics, contract risk detection) layered onto a unified CLM+sourcing+P2P suite, but the AI story has never been presented to the buyer because no demo occurred. The 'how we win' is to position Merlin as the Phase 2 AI layer that none of the competitors offer at this maturity level, but that story is dead in the water without buyer engagement. Upon re-engagement, the discovery call must include competitive positioning: 'I know you are evaluating multiple vendors. What are the key differences you are seeing, and what matters most as you compare them?' Then demo Merlin as the differentiator.

identify_pain.narrative: Operational pain is referenced but not deeply qualified or quantified. The 2026-03-23 Salesforce task note from Melissa Geddie mentioned 'procurement transformation' and 'contract lifecycle management', implying dissatisfaction with the current state (likely manual contract authoring, decentralized supplier management, lack of spend visibility, slow approval cycles). However, no discovery call occurred to extract the specific pain: How many contracts are authored per year? What is the average approval cycle time? What is the error rate or compliance risk in the current process? What is the cost of maverick spend or supplier consolidation opportunity? Who owns this pain internally (General Counsel for CLM, CFO for spend visibility, Procurement Director for supplier management)? The pain is inferred from the product scope (iContract CLM, iSource sourcing, eProcurement P2P, Merlin AI negotiation), but it has never been articulated by the buyer with specificity or emotional weight. With 100 days dark, the pain may have been deprioritized (other projects took precedence), the budget may have been pulled (cost containment), or the pain may have been addressed another way (incumbent vendor upgrade, internal process fix, competitor selection). The discovery call must open with pain validation: 'Tell me about the procurement challenges that prompted this evaluation. What is broken today, what is it costing you, and what happens if you do nothing?'

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement channel, and InfoSec requirements are entirely unknown. No MSA (Master Service Agreement) or SOW (Statement of Work) discussion has occurred. No InfoSec questionnaire, data privacy review, or ERP integration scoping session is documented. No System Integrator or implementation partner is mentioned. For a four-product enterprise procurement suite (CLM, sourcing, P2P, AI negotiation) at a law firm, the paper process typically includes: (1) InfoSec and data-privacy review (attorney-client privilege, document retention, cloud security attestations), (2) IT review for integration with existing ERP/finance systems (likely Elite, NetSuite, or SAP), (3) Procurement channel (direct buy vs preferred vendor list vs consortium), (4) Legal redlining of MSA (liability caps, indemnification, data ownership, AI/ML model transparency clauses for Merlin), (5) SOW scoping for implementation (data migration, user training, phased rollout across offices). No Current_Contract_Expiration__c date is populated, so there is no forcing function from an incumbent renewal. With 100 days dark and no contracting visibility, even if the buyer re-engages and selects Zycus, the paper process could add 60-90 days, pushing close beyond 27 Aug. The discovery call must map the contracting path: 'What does your contracting process look like for a SaaS procurement platform? Who in Legal and IT will review, and what are the typical sticking points?'

A $90k enterprise procurement suite (iContract CLM, iSource sourcing, eProcurement P2P, Merlin AI) requires CFO or Chief Procurement Officer budget approval at a law firm of Jackson Lewis PC's scale (650+ attorneys, AmLaw 200). The only contact on record is Melissa Geddie, Sr. Manager Procurement with 'Evaluator' role — an operational gatekeeper who can champion internally but cannot sign off on a six-figure investment. No CFO, no CPO, no COO, no Managing Partner is linked as a contact role, and no Avoma call transcript references budget authority or executive sponsorship. The 2026-07-01 Next Step shows the rep probing Melissa for project priority, but even if she responds, she cannot close without C-level approval. Without EB access, this deal is un-closeable. The next move upon any buyer re-engagement is to explicitly request an introduction to the budget owner ('Who controls the FY26 procurement technology budget and will sign off on this investment?') and to position the conversation as an executive business review, not a vendor pitch.
